"Arcwright's testimony begins at 1800," Iris added. "Eleven hours and eight minutes."

"When the testimony begins, the authorization window opens. The sequence initiates. The border station relay broadcasts the transition authorization through the monitoring network." Cross paused. "I need all three arrays for that broadcast."

"Understood," Lian said.

Cross sat down on the pre-collapse floor of the ninth chamber, beside the assembled array, and waited.

---

Three hours and twelve minutes until the contact-loss protocol expired.

The monitoring display showed eleven signatures. Vessa Korr had not moved from the bench at the western wall. The crystal's lattice ran the 47.2-omega in the amber light, and in the ninth chamber below the station walk — now assembled, now waiting — the array was ready for a sequence that would not initiate for eleven more hours.

And in three hours and twelve minutes, the eighteen Null at the relay arrays would proceed with their mission unless Corin found a way to stop them that did not involve Vessa giving an order she had not yet decided to give.

chapters.title: Chapter 6: Arcwright's Request
chapters.text:
Sevin Harr arrived at the holding room at 0547.

She had been constructing the operational timeline since the first monitoring alert — 0514, branded heretic in inner perimeter, secondary response team deployed; 0518, twelve unclassified individuals confirmed inside inner perimeter, escort to processing; 0527, monitoring display discrepancy in main holding room, eleven signatures for thirteen occupants; 0531, low-priority credential alert from the side corridor reader, Valen Cross's credentials, Section 7 documentation.

The last item. She had been sitting with it for sixteen minutes.

She pulled the pre-collapse floor plan from the facility archive — the survey record that Cross had filed fifteen years ago, a routine calibration equipment audit that had been filed and not reviewed since its filing date. The side corridor: forty-one meters, connecting the maintenance wing junction to the station walk access. The station walk: forty meters from the side corridor junction to the primary entrance, sixty meters to the pre-collapse sub-section, ninety meters to the end of the documented layout.

The pre-collapse sub-section extended past the documented layout. She could see it in the floor plan — the chambers that the Archive's current documentation did not include because including them would have required someone to document what was in them, and the Archive's documentation was built on what had been reviewed and authorized, not on what existed.

The ninth chamber was in the floor plan. It was not in the Archive's current records.

She applied her credentials to the holding room door.

---

The room had thirteen people. The monitoring showed eleven. She looked at the display for two seconds, then at the room, then at the display. Eight years of inner perimeter security. She had developed a functional relationship with the monitoring's limitations — knew what it missed, knew when the miss was a calibration issue and when it was something else. This was something else. Twelve people in the room were not producing Resonance signatures because they had removed every piece of technology that produced them. The monitoring was working correctly. There was simply nothing for it to detect.

She sat in the chair beside the door. Data pad on her knee.

"I'm conducting an initial assessment." She looked at Corin first. "The side corridor credential reader logged Valen Cross's authorization at 0531. The side corridor connects to the station walk sub-section. The station walk's pre-collapse chambers include" — she checked the floor plan on her pad — "a ninth chamber that is not in the Archive's current facility records." She paused. "The array components are in the ninth chamber."

"They were moved during your response team's deployment to the perimeter breach point," Corin said. "While the primary entrance was unguarded."

She looked at him. "You walked north to pull the response team from the entrance."

"Yes."

"Ten minutes of unguarded primary entrance. Twelve crates at pace through the maintenance tunnel and side corridor. Eight minutes." She ran the calculation. "Two minutes margin."

"Yes."

She looked at the Congregation members. At Vessa Korr, watching the exchange with the same assessment quality she had shown throughout. "The eighteen at the relay arrays. Contact-loss protocol."

"Three hours and nine minutes remaining," Vessa said. "Contact was lost at entry. The clock started at 0504."

Sevin Harr looked at the time. 0547. 0504 plus three hours nine minutes: 0813 expiration. "If the eighteen dismantle the relay arrays, the border station relay coverage falls below the transition authorization threshold."

"Yes," Corin said.

She looked at the monitoring display showing eleven signatures. "Arcwright requested to be informed when you were secured. He's in the testimony preparation suite. The security director is with him." She stood. "I need to brief the security director on the contact-loss protocol situation. The conversation needs to happen now. Would you rather be in the room?"

---

The testimony preparation suite was two levels up: standard amber lighting, Archive furniture, a monitoring display that showed two signatures when Sevin Harr opened the door and fifteen people walked in. The security director — Hollan, four years in the position, with the specific expression of someone who had been briefed on the Congregation's monitoring profile but not on the holding room's full composition — processed the display discrepancy in the same way she had, then looked at Sevin Harr with an expression that said he had understood and was already working through the operational implications.

Arcwright was at the table.

Seventy-one years old. The specific quality of a man who had been doing a specific thing for so long that the doing had become the entirety of what he was — not what he had been before, not what he might be after, but the continuous present act of it. For four months, the act had been testimony. He looked at Corin when the door opened, and his expression had the quality of something that had been held in place for a long time and was now being set down.

"I have been wanting to speak to you since the partial deployment," he said. "Since the monitoring registered the twelve-second response and the system logged the frequency as a non-standard harmonic output." He paused. "Not the twelve seconds specifically. Before that. Since Cross's credentials appeared in the Section 7 suppression review and I understood what you were attempting to do."

Corin stood across the table from the man who had signed the suppression authorization for his father's research. Who had signed the declination when Aldric Draven presented 47.2-omega to the Council. Whose name was at the end of every authorization chain in the research archive that Corin had spent four months working backward through.

"You read the research," Corin said.

Arcwright looked at him. "Yes."

"Before you signed the declination."

"Yes." He paused. "I understood what the frequency was. I understood it was additive, not extractive. I understood that your father had spent seventeen years designing something that was the inverse of everything the Archive's calibration program was built on." He paused again. "I signed the declination. Not because the research was wrong. Because the research was correct — specifically and demonstrably correct — and the Archive's position was that Resonance access was authorized only for calibration purposes, and an additive healing frequency was not a calibration purpose, and my job was to enforce the Archive's position."

"You knew it could work," Corin said. "You knew the entity was in pain and the research addressed the pain directly and you declined it anyway."

"I declined it because accepting it would have required the Archive to acknowledge that the entity was in pain. That the calibration program was producing harm. That the institution I had been running for thirty years was built on harm as its operational foundation." He looked at the table. "I declined it because I did not want to know that."

The testimony preparation suite was quiet.

Arcwright looked at the crystal in Corin's hand. The 47.2-omega lattice running at low intensity, the same frequency he had read in the research application twelve years ago and understood and declined. He had not seen the crystal before — Aldric had presented the research to the Council as a formal application, not with physical demonstration equipment. But the frequency pattern the lattice was transmitting was recognizable. Not from memory specifically. From the research documentation he had been reading, in the testimony preparation process, for four months. He had been reading his own archive. His own files. The specific research he had suppressed, in the voice of the person who had spent seventeen years producing it.

He looked at the crystal and said nothing.

Hollan had been listening. He looked at Sevin Harr. "The contact-loss protocol. Options."

"Dispatch outer perimeter security to the relay array positions. But the Congregation's spokesperson" — she glanced at Vessa — "says the eighteen will respond to security presence as a threat. No communication channel means no way to convey instructions."

"The alternative," Corin said, "is releasing one of the twelve to carry a message. One person, escort to the outer perimeter, ten minutes."

Hollan looked at Vessa. "What does the message authorize specifically?"

"Hold the arrays," Corin said. "Don't dismantle until the deployment completes. If the deployment produces harm — if the entity's response to the full frequency matches extraction patterns rather than healing patterns — the Congregation has its evidence and the relay arrays are not relevant to anything afterward. If the deployment completes without harm, the Congregation can dismantle them then, if they choose."

Vessa looked at him. At Arcwright, who had been watching her since she entered the room. At the monitoring display that showed two signatures for fifteen people.

"Send Renn," she said. She indicated the man with the mended clothing. "The fastest runner we have. He knows the arrays' positions. He knows the stop signal."

"What does the stop signal authorize specifically?" Hollan asked.

"Hold position," Vessa said. "Pending further instruction from me." She paused. "I give further instruction when the deployment has completed and the entity's response has been recorded. Not before."

Hollan processed this. The security director's calculus was visible — the risk of releasing a custody subject from inner perimeter holding under emergency security protocols against the operational need to preserve the relay arrays for the next eleven hours. He looked at Sevin Harr. She looked at the monitoring display.

"I'll authorize the release," he said. "Conditional: outer perimeter escort maintained until return. No inner perimeter access." He was already reaching for the supplementary authorization form. The specific form for releasing inner perimeter custody subjects under conditional protocols, which existed because the Archive's institutional response to non-standard situations was always to create documentation for them.

The authorization was processed at 0611.

Renn was through the Citadel's outer perimeter exit at 0614, flanked by two outer perimeter security officers, moving north.

The relay array positions on the northern face were at the end of the outer perimeter's northern approach road. Walking distance: approximately ninety minutes at a standard pace. Renn moved faster than standard pace. The contact-loss clock had two hours and fifty-eight minutes remaining at 0614.

What the standard mapping did not account for: the northern approach road passed through the dead zone's eastern edge, where the Congregation had spent three days systematically dismantling monitoring equipment. The Congregation dismantled carefully — components stacked in organized piles, not scattered — but the road's navigable surface required careful foot routing through the debris. The escort team was not trained for terrain navigation without monitoring support.

Renn was. He waited at each debris point while the escort team worked through the navigation. The clock ran.

He reached the first array position at 0756. Two minutes before expiration.

The first group — six of the eighteen, positioned at the northernmost relay array — had been watching the clock. They received the signal at 0756. They held.

Renn moved to the second group. One minute until expiration.

The second group received the signal at 0758. One minute after the clock expired.

In the sixty seconds between the clock expiring and the signal arriving, one of the six — a woman with seventeen years of careful equipment dismantling, her hands moving in the practiced sequence of someone who had done this work in precisely this way across hundreds of monitoring installations — had removed the first three mounting bolts from the northernmost relay array and was reaching for a fourth. The bolts were removed cleanly. The array was still functional. The transmission coil connection was intact.

She stopped when Renn arrived. She looked at the three bolts in her hand. At the array, still standing, still transmitting. At Renn.

"Hold," he said.

She held. She did not put the bolts back. They were in her hand. She stood in front of the partially-dismantled relay array in the early morning light and held position.

The third group received the signal at 0801, before any dismantling had begun.

Two relay arrays were completely intact. One relay array was standing with three mounting bolts removed and an outer casing fastener the woman had also loosened before Renn arrived.

---

Cross's assessment, when Lian relayed the status at 0812: the compromised array was still transmitting. The border station relay field coverage registered at 71%. The transition authorization protocol required coverage above 60% at the border station for viable transmission. The designed operational standard — the threshold the protocol had been built around — was 85%.

71% was viable. It was not reliable. In standard operational conditions, a coverage reading of 71% would trigger a maintenance notification and a recommendation to defer any pending authorizations until the coverage was restored to standard.

These were not standard operational conditions.

Cross was in the ninth chamber when Lian's relay came through. He looked at the assembled array — the copper Fibonacci spiral sections, the 47.2-omega crystal in the interface housing, the pre-collapse indicator steady at its blue-adjacent baseline. The array was ready. The authorization window would open in eleven hours and one minute when Arcwright's testimony began.

Eleven hours. During which the partially-dismantled relay array's compromised structure would sit on the northern face of the Citadel subject to wind load and thermal expansion and the specific structural degradation that occurred when three mounting bolts had been removed from a transmission component that was designed to be secured by seven.

"What's the wind forecast for the northern face?" he said.

"The monitoring doesn't maintain wind data for the outer perimeter," Lian said.

He was quiet. The coverage indicator on his relay device showed 71%. The minimum threshold was 60%. He had eleven percentage points of margin, a mechanically compromised antenna structure, eleven hours to wait, and no way to monitor whether the wind would move the loosened components enough to degrade the connection before the authorization window opened.

"I'll proceed with the sequence when the window opens," he said. "The authorization protocol requires 60% minimum. We are above that threshold."

"Are you saying that to me or to yourself?"

Cross looked at the assembled array in the ninth chamber. At the data crystal seated in the housing — Aldric Draven's seventeen years, the inverse of every calibration parameter the Archive had ever authorized. At the pre-collapse indicator light, steady and waiting.

He had filed the storage authorization for this equipment fifteen years ago. He had processed the form without reading what was in the crates, because reading the contents was not required by the authorization procedure. He had spent fifteen years running the calibration program while Aldric's healing array sat in a storage room sixty meters away. He had extracted field coverage reports for that storage room's ambient monitoring data. He had never looked at what was being stored there. That was the specific form of the Archive's harm — built into the institutional structure so completely that the people who perpetuated it did not need to choose to perpetuate it. They only needed to not look.

He had spent fifteen years not looking.

"Both," he said.

chapters.title: Chapter 7: The Authorization Window
chapters.text:
The testimony began at 1800.

Ren Arcwright walked into the Council chamber — the same chamber where the Council of Seven had met for eighty years, its obsidian walls and amber lighting unchanged since the last authorized erasure — and sat in the witness position. Not the Council table. The witness position, at the chamber's center, facing the presiding panel of three restored representatives who had been selected by the reconciliation assembly to receive testimony.

Corin was in the observer section. To his left, Hollan. To his right, two administrative staff. Behind the observer section, Vessa Korr and three of the Congregation members Hollan had authorized to be present — he had made the operational judgment that having the Congregation's representatives witness the testimony directly was preferable to having them hear a secondhand account.

Lian was in the service bay, monitoring the relay array coverage status on the secondary display terminal.

Cross was in the ninth chamber, waiting for the testimony confirmation that would open the authorization window.

Iris was watching via the Timelost network relay from the administration hub — the same shipping-container complex where she had been documenting erased lives for decades, the wall maps still expanding, her ink-stained fingers moving across the data pad with seven days of evacuation coordination in her voice.

The presiding representative — a woman named Cara from the Teller territory restoration commission — opened the session. The chamber's monitoring registered the standard occupancy signatures. Vessa Korr and the three Congregation members were in the observer section. The monitoring showed nothing in the positions they occupied. Cara had been briefed on the monitoring profile. She did not look at the display.

"Ren Arcwright. You have requested to testify before this assembly. The record is active. You may begin."

---

"My name is Ren Arcwright," he said. "I served as the Archive's Primary Archivist for thirty years. In that capacity, I authorized eleven thousand four hundred and seven timeline erasures. I signed six research declinations for projects that I believed were technically valid and institutionally inconvenient. I suppressed the testimony of five apprentice Chronometrists who had identified harm in the Archive's calibration program and reported it through standard channels." He paused. "I am testifying because I requested to testify. Not because I was asked. Because the record needs to include what I authorized and why, and the record needs to include it in my voice."

The chamber was quiet. The monitoring showed the standard occupancy signatures — Corin's Echo prominent as the only branded-heretic designation in the room, the presiding panel's Archive credentials, the administrative staff.

Thirty meters below, in the ninth chamber, the testimony confirmation arrived on Cross's control interface unit — not a signal from the Archive's network, which he was not connected to, but from the border station relay confirmation that Elara Venn's deliberation protection had held open for this specific moment. The authorization window was open.

He initiated the deployment sequence.

---

The 47.2-omega frequency left the copper Fibonacci spiral array at 47.2 cycles per omega-wave — the specific frequency that Aldric Draven had identified across seventeen years of parameter refinement as the resonant complement to the entity's wound. Not calibrated against extraction efficiency. Calibrated against the entity's specific harmonic profile, the way a treatment was calibrated against the condition it addressed rather than against the equipment's output capacity.

The frequency moved through the ninth chamber's walls into the geological substrate. Not detectable by the Archive's monitoring — below every threshold calibrated for Archive Resonance extraction equipment, invisible to the Reckoner network, invisible to the field coverage arrays running the inner perimeter's standard protocols.

Not invisible to the entity.

The entity was in the substrate. Had been for eight centuries. The specific quality of its reaching — the frequency shifting from held-position to extending — that Corin had first noticed in the sub-basement, that Cross had registered in the ninth chamber when the array was assembled, that had been building since the crystal was seated in the housing — increased.

Corin felt it in the observer section. Not the physical sensation of Resonance activation — not the way a calibration plate felt when it was operating, the humming that a Chronometrist with full access would feel in their hands and chest. Something different. The specific quality of a frequency that the entity was receiving rather than a frequency it was being subjected to. Receiving the way a wound received an appropriate treatment — not the absence of sensation but the sensation of something moving correctly rather than against the structure of the thing receiving it.

The entity had been in pain for eight centuries. The stopping half had been screaming the pain into every Resonance channel the calibration program monitored, and the calibration program had never been designed to listen — only to extract, to measure, to optimize. The 47.2-omega was the first frequency in eight centuries that was designed to do something other than take. The entity's response was not to a good feeling. It was to the absence of the specific harm that every other frequency contact had been producing. After eight centuries, the absence of harm felt like something.

He looked at Arcwright, who was continuing his testimony.

---

"I want to address the Research Program 17-X authorizations specifically," Arcwright said. "The Section 7 cooperative facility was established under Councilor Dessa Parren's oversight authority. The contractors who operated the facility were selected from the Archive's external affairs network. The facility's function was the suppression of Resonance research that the Council had determined to be institutionally inconvenient." He paused. "I authorized the Research Program 17-X contracting process. I reviewed the facility's function. I signed the suppression orders for the five apprentice Chronometrists whose research had been flagged as non-compliant."

Cara looked up from the record. "Including the 47.2-omega research."

"Including Aldric Draven's 47.2-omega project." Arcwright looked at his hands. "I read the 47.2-omega research before I signed the suppression authorization. I read it completely. I understood what the frequency was — an additive harmonic designed specifically to return energy to the entity rather than extract it. I understood that the research was not Archive-compliant because it required acknowledging that the entity was in a state of harm that the Archive's calibration program had produced and was perpetuating." He paused. "I signed the suppression authorization because acknowledging that harm was institutionally incompatible with the calibration program's continued operation. I knew that. I made that choice explicitly. And the entity continued bleeding for fifteen more years."

The chamber was quiet.

In the service bay, Lian's monitoring display updated: coverage at 70%. Down from 71% since 0812. Not a catastrophic decline — single-digit, consistent with normal antenna performance variance over a twelve-hour period. But the loosened outer casing fastener on the compromised array was not normal antenna variance. It was mechanical degradation from incomplete dismantling, and the northern face of the Citadel's outer wall was experiencing the specific thermal contraction that occurred when a surface that had absorbed a full day of direct exposure began to cool.

70% gave her ten percentage points of margin. The minimum threshold was 60%. The deployment sequence was running.

She did not relay the update to Cross. He was in the ninth chamber, in a pre-collapse space below monitoring coverage, running a deployment sequence from a device that she could not ping without the monitoring infrastructure she was watching but not connected to. She noted the coverage reading in her log.

---

"I want to address the eastern field calibration," Arcwright said.

The specific silence of a testimony chamber when a subject reaches the specific thing that the chamber has been waiting for: not louder, not more charged with visible emotion — quieter. The quality of people who are listening with a different quality of attention.

"Thirty-one years ago. The calibration efficiency trial in the eastern territories. I signed the deployment authorization." He looked at the record. "The trial's purpose was to test increased yield parameters for the field extraction arrays in low-density coverage zones. The affected zone in the eastern territories was selected because its population density was classified as low in the Archive's demographic monitoring data. Low-density zones were standard test sites for efficiency trials because the yield measurements were simpler to analyze without the interference patterns that occurred in high-density urban monitoring environments."

"Was the affected zone's population notified?" Cara asked.

"No. Field efficiency trials were classified as internal calibration operations. Notification was not a requirement of the calibration operations protocol. The protocol had been in place for twenty-two years. No field efficiency trial had ever included a population notification." He paused. "The eastern territories had a population of approximately eight hundred people in the affected coverage zone. The monitoring classified them as low-density. The classification was accurate by the Archive's metric. The metric measured Resonance signature density, which was lower in the eastern territories because the population had a different relationship to the monitoring infrastructure than the Archive's primary territory populations."

"What does that mean?" the second presiding representative asked — a man from the Waymark settlement network who had joined the reconciliation assembly in the third month.

Arcwright looked at him. "The eastern territory populations had lower Resonance output because a significant proportion of them were not using Archive-registered technology. Not because there were fewer people. Because they were already moving toward what the Congregation would later formalize — the reduction of Resonance-emitting technology in their communities." He paused. "The Archive's monitoring counted them as low-density because the monitoring measured signatures, not people. They were not low-density. They were deliberately quieter. The Archive's metric failed to distinguish between the two."

Vessa Korr was in the observer section. Corin looked at her. She was watching Arcwright with an expression he had not seen on her face before — not the assessment quality, not the specific Congregation leader's reading of a situation. Something older and more precise. The expression of someone who had been carrying a story for thirty-one years and had just heard the version that included the detail explaining why the Archive had selected the eastern territories in the first place.

"The yield anomaly," Arcwright said. "The field calibration produced a yield anomaly in its fourth hour. An unexpected resonance response from the substrate layer — the geological formation beneath the eastern territories conducts Resonance differently than the standard formations under the Archive's primary territories. The extraction interfaced with the substrate's natural frequency and amplified the extraction intensity beyond the planned parameters." He paused. "The anomaly was logged as a data spike in the extraction readings. It was not logged as a connection to the human population in the affected zone. The distinction between a substrate anomaly and a population event is the kind of distinction that the calibration program's protocol did not make, because the calibration program's protocol was designed for efficiency optimization and not for human impact assessment."

"What did the anomaly produce?" Cara asked.

"It produced the permanent stripping of the Resonance capacity of approximately two hundred and sixteen individuals in the affected zone. Not timeline erasure — the Archive's erasure procedure removes a person from their temporal position but preserves the underlying Resonance architecture for later restoration. What the eastern field calibration produced was the removal of the Resonance architecture itself. The foundational frequency structure that anchors a person to a timeline. The capacity that makes restoration possible." He looked at the table. "The two hundred and sixteen are outside the restoration architecture entirely. The entity cannot recognize their signatures because the signatures do not exist. They are not in the Timelost registry. They are not in the erased-timeline records. They exist as a yield anomaly notation in the calibration trial documentation and as two hundred and sixteen people who will never be restored regardless of what the restoration program achieves."

The chamber was quiet in the specific way that chambers were quiet when testimony put a specific number on a specific harm for the first time in an official record.

"The fourteen people in the restoration queue," Vessa said from the observer section.

Cara looked at her. The presiding representative's expression held the specific deliberation of someone who was deciding whether to enforce procedural boundaries or let the testimony chamber contain the exchange that was happening.

She let it continue.

"The fourteen people in the Archive's restoration queue at the time of the calibration," Vessa said. "They continued to wait for four days. They sent messages home. The Archive did not review the queue after the calibration to update their status. No one connected the calibration data to the queue entries."

"I know," Arcwright said.

"Who decided not to update the queue?"

"No one decided. The queue was automated. The calibration was a separate department. The protocol adjustment was filed with the trial report. The connection was not made." He paused. "I was the Primary Archivist. The protocol adjustment filing went through my office. I signed the efficiency trial report. I did not review the restoration queue for the affected zone. I did not ask whether the affected zone had any active queue entries. I did not connect the calibration data to the people waiting for their appointments." He paused. "Not malice. Not deliberate harm. The specific form of harm that comes from an institution that has built a system for managing lives and has stopped noticing that the entries in the system are people."

The chamber was very quiet.

In the service bay, Lian's coverage display updated again: 69%. The thermal contraction cycle on the northern face was continuing. The loosened fastener was the specific kind of structural vulnerability that maintenance engineers identified during routine inspection and flagged for same-day repair, because a single loosened component in a directional relay antenna was not a failure — it was the precondition for a failure that would happen at the worst possible time under the most inconvenient environmental conditions.

There were currently no maintenance engineers on the northern face. The eighteen members of the Null Congregation were on the northern face.

She noted the reading in the log.

"The 47.2-omega," Arcwright said. He looked at Corin. "Your father designed the frequency for the entity's wound. Seventeen years of research. The inverse of the calibration program's extraction logic." He paused. "He designed it for the wound. Not for the specific category of harm the eastern field calibration produced." He looked at the observer section. "Did he design it for the unrestorable? For the two hundred and sixteen outside the restoration architecture? Is there anything in the 47.2-omega that could address the harm the calibration trial produced — the permanent stripping, not the erasure?"

Corin looked at Arcwright.

The Council chamber was quiet. The monitoring showed standard occupancy signatures. In the ninth chamber, the deployment sequence was running through the copper Fibonacci spiral array, the frequency moving through the geological substrate toward an entity that had been waiting for it for eight centuries.

He did not answer immediately, because the honest answer required the space to be heard correctly.

chapters.title: Chapter 8: What the Stopping Half Heard
chapters.text:
"I don't know," Corin said.

The Council chamber held the specific silence of a room that had been waiting for an answer and had received the one it did not expect.

"The 47.2-omega was designed for the entity's wound," he said. "The wound is the fracture — the split between the healing half and the stopping half, eight centuries ago, when the extraction began. My father's seventeen years identified that fracture as the damage requiring treatment. The frequency is calibrated to address that fracture — to return energy to a system that has been depleted by extraction, to offer the entity a frequency it can receive as healing rather than harm." He paused. "The two hundred and sixteen are a different category of harm. The field calibration did not fracture the entity's structure. It stripped the Resonance architecture of two hundred and sixteen people — removed the anchoring capacity that allows the entity to recognize and restore a timeline. That is not the wound the 47.2-omega was designed for."

"Then it can't reach them," Vessa said.

"I said I don't know." He looked at her. "The 47.2-omega is additive. It returns energy to a system that has been depleted. Whether an additive frequency can reconstitute a Resonance architecture that was stripped — not suspended, not fractured, stripped — is a question my father's research did not address, because the field calibration wasn't known to him the way it is known to us now." He paused. "I am not saying it can. I am saying that I cannot confirm it cannot."

Vessa looked at him for the specific length of time required to assess whether the distinction between "cannot confirm it cannot" and "believe it can" had operational significance.

"You're saying there is a possibility," she said.

"A possibility that was not designed for and has not been tested. Not a probability. A possibility."

The chamber was quiet. Arcwright was watching the exchange. Cara and the presiding panel were watching. The three Congregation members in the observer section were watching.
